message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 11
Recent Green phase: added moonstone:25 to BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll execute the Refactor phase systematically. Let me start by loading the architecture memory and reviewing the current state.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping

node_modules

pnpm-lock.yaml
prompt.md
run.log
src
tdd-journal.md
tsconfig.json
vitest.config.ts

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` in `src/claim-office.ts`; `quote()` now returns `PROCESSING_FEE`
5	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `5` is the domain "processing fee" from the spec; naming it removes a magic number
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant); `quote(input)` public function kept as-is — name still fits
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- No refactoring (justified): only 2 data points (empty=5, single potion newcomer=49); the literal `49` is hardcoded and any decomposition into `base + first-insurance + fee` components would be speculative triangulation beyond what tests demand. Naming `49` (e.g. `POTION_NEWCOMER_PREMIUM`) would not add intent beyond the test name and would clutter the module before the algorithm emerges in cycle 3+ (amulet test). `quote` name still fits.
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
13	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
14	- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- Change: decomposed opaque per-type totals into the domain formula. `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (49/71/93) → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (40/60/80) plus new constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. `quote` now computes `basePrice + basePrice * 0.1 + PROCESSING_FEE` for non-empty item lists.
18	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — with the 3rd data point (staff 80→93) in place, all three test names explicitly state the same `base + first-insurance + fee` decomposition; the implementation now mirrors the spec language instead of hiding the formula behind precomputed totals. Also Rule 3 (DRY): the surcharge+fee pattern was conceptually duplicated across all three map entries; now expressed once. Mass increased ~25→~33 — accepted because Rule 2 > APP. Verified all 3 base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword case (100) yield integer results under `base*0.1`, so no rounding decision was committed (rounding rule for rune 25→33 deferred to its own cycle).
19	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (replaces `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`), `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (new domain constant); `quote` name still fits
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): sword test added one row (`sword: 100`) to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` — the cycle-4 decomposition absorbed it without any structural change. Considered inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * (1 + RATE)` for Rule 4 (fewest elements), rejected because test names enumerate three distinct components ("100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee") and Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP. Considered extracting a `priceForFirstItem(type)` helper, rejected as speculative — no second-item or modifier test active yet. `quote` name still fits.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` so the final return reads `return Math.ceil(premium)` instead of `Math.ceil(basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE)`. Also added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (in Green); the structural fit was already there.
26	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the rune test introduced rounding-up as a domain rule (25 × 1.1 + 5 = 32.5 → 33). Naming `premium` makes the rounding step a visible, named operation ("round the premium up to a whole gold piece") rather than a wrapper around a raw arithmetic expression. Mass +1 binding (~35→36), accepted on the cycle-4 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when test vocabulary ("premium ... rounded up") explicitly names the value. Considered leaving the inline form for Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected because the rounding rule's target deserves a name now that rounding exists. Considered renaming `quote` itself (e.g. to `computePremium`) — rejected, "quote" is the domain action and the function still returns the quoted premium amount.
27	- Naming established: `premium` (local binding for the pre-rounded total); `quote` and existing constants unchanged.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: removed the empty-items early-return guard (`if (input.items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;`). With cycle-7 Green generalising `basePrice` to `items.reduce(..., 0)`, the empty case now falls out of the same formula: `0 + 0*0.1 + 5 = 5` (and `Math.ceil(5) = 5`). One uniform code path handles all item counts.
31	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the special case became dead code the moment `basePrice` became an accumulator over `items`. Also nets Rule 2 (reveals intent): a single formula expresses the domain rule "premium = ceil(sum-of-base-prices × 1.1 + processing fee)" for every item-count, rather than splitting "empty" and "non-empty" branches. Mass drop ~−9 (one conditional, one length access, one equality, one literal `0`). Considered keeping the guard for "documentation value" — rejected: the empty test name ("empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)") is now an emergent consequence of the general formula, which is stronger evidence of correctness than a hand-coded shortcut. Naming all still fits.
32	- Naming established: none new; removed elements only.
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- Change: extracted the literal `60` in the 3-runes guard into module-level constant `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`. Body now reads `isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice` instead of `... ? 60 : rawBasePrice`. Implementation shape (narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes`) left intact.
36	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `60` is the domain "building block price for 3 runes" per Example Mapping; naming removes a magic number and the visual collision with `amulet: 60` (different concepts, same value). Mass +1 (one new constant binding at module scope), accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `hasRuneBuildingBlock` and generalising to a per-block discount (would scale to cycles 9-12: 4 runes, 7 runes, mixed rune/moonstone). Rejected as speculative — next test ("4 runes → no block") will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation. The narrow guard's name remains accurate for the single data point we have. Considered also extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named predicate function — rejected, it's used once and inlining preserves locality.
37	- Naming established: `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant); `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `quote`, and other existing names unchanged.
38	
39	## After cycle 9
40	- Change: extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to remove three repeated `input.items` accesses across the reduce, the length check, and the every-call. Body now reads `items.reduce(...)`, `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`.
41	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) — three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~−3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change — the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it — confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. Considered (and rejected) generalising the rune-block logic to `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks now; that is speculative triangulation. The 7-runes cycle (cycle 10, currently `it.todo` "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base") is the genuine forcing test for that generalisation. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes`: name still accurate after a negative-case confirmation. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function or inlining `rawBasePrice`: both rejected on cycle-8 precedent (locality / verbose-expression-deserves-a-name respectively).
42	- Naming established: `items` (local binding aliasing `input.items`); no other name changes.
43	
44	## After cycle 10
45	- No refactoring (justified): the 7-runes test (expected base 175 = 7 × 25, premium 198) passed without any implementation change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `items.length === 3` excludes the 7-rune case from the block-discount path, so `rawBasePrice` (175) flows straight through `basePrice` and the existing formula yields ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = ceil(197.5) = 198. Important observation: the test's prose ("1 block of 3 + 4 individuals → 175 G base") describes the *narrative* but the *numeric expectation* (175 = 7 × 25, no discount) confirms that the block discount only applies when exactly 3 runes are insured — making this the SECOND confirming test for the narrow guard, not a forcing test. The genuine forcing test for `floor(runeCount/3)` generalisation is cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base"), where two distinct per-type blocks must each be priced at 60. Generalising now (computing `floor(count/3)` blocks across all runes only) would still need restructuring once moonstones arrive, so it's premature. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock` (consequence-oriented over structure-oriented, marginal Rule 2 gain) — rejected because cycle 12 will force the predicate to change shape entirely (per-type rune+moonstone block counting), so any rename now is churn. Considered inlining `rawBasePrice` into the ternary or collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * 1.1` — both rejected on cycle-4/cycle-5 precedent that test-name vocabulary ("base + first-insurance + fee") earns each value its own named binding (Rule 2 > APP). Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function — rejected on cycle-8 precedent (used once, inlining preserves locality). All existing names still fit.
46

content.text:
All 11 tests are green. Now let me execute the refactor evaluation systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

**Current names**:
- `quote` — public function. Domain action: produce a quote/premium. Still fits.
- `items` — local alias. Clear.
- `rawBasePrice` — sum of base prices without any block discount. Fits.
- `isThreeAlikeRunes` — predicate for 3 identical runes. **Critical evaluation**: with cycle 11 just added ("2 runes + 1 moonstone → no block"), this is the second negative-case confirmation. The predicate is still factually correct for the rune-only path. But cycle 12 (next test, `it.todo`) will introduce "3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks", which will force the per-type block logic. Renaming now would be churn — same reasoning as cycles 9 and 10.
- `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium` — all match spec vocabulary. Still fit.
- `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` — all domain-named.

**Decision**: No rename — `isThreeAlikeRunes` will be restructured by cycle 12; renaming pre-emptively is speculative.

## Step 2: Calculate Current APP Mass

```
Module-level:
- 4 constants (PROCESSING_FEE=5, FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE=0.1, THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE=60, 6-entry record with 6 literals)
  Bindings: 4 + 6 keys = 10 (count keys as bindings) → simpler: constants 11 (5, 0.1, 60, 40, 60, 80, 100, 25, 25, plus 2 from record syntax — call it ~10), bindings ~4

quote() body:
- bindings: items, rawBasePrice, isThreeAlikeRunes, basePrice, firstInsuranceSurcharge, premium, sum, item, item (in every) = 9 bindings = 9
- invocations: reduce, BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[item.type] (subscript ~1), +, length access, every, item.type === "rune", ternary uses ?, *, +, +, Math.ceil
  Count function-like calls: reduce(1), every(1), Math.ceil(1) = 3 invocations × 2 = 6
- conditionals: ternary (?: ) = 1 × 4 = 4; && in length check = 1 conditional × 4 = 4; === in every = doesn't count as conditional in APP strictly, but treat as logical → 0
  Total conditionals: ~2 × 4 = 8
- loops: reduce(1), every(1) = 2 × 5 = 10
- assignments: 0 (all are const bindings, no mutations) = 0
- constants in body: 0, "rune", 3 = 3 × 1 = 3

Rough total mass: ~40
```

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests pass)**: ✅ 11/11 passing.

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent)**: 
- The expression `items.length === 3 && items.every((item) => item.type === "rune")` reads well. 
- Cycle 11 added the moonstone case which tests confirmed works because the every-check requires all items to be the same type "rune". This is correct, but with moonstone now in `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, one could ask: should the predicate generalize? The answer per architecture notes: NO, cycle 12 is the forcing test.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**:
- No obvious duplication in current code.
- The `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` record: `rune: 25, moonstone: 25` — same value, but conceptually they're different item types that happen to share a price. Extracting `RUNE_COMPONENT_PRICE = 25` would couple them inappropriately; cycle 12 will reveal whether they share block-rules too.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements)**: 
- All current elements serve a purpose.
- Could inline `rawBasePrice` into the ternary: rejected per cycle-4/5/10 precedent.
- Could inline `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * 1.1`: rejected per cycle-5 precedent.

## Step 4: Refactoring Candidate Evaluation

Let me carefully look for any improvement opportunity:

**Candidate A**: Extract `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named function `qualifiesForRuneBlock(items)` — Rejected on cycle-8/10 precedent (used once, locality; cycle 12 will restructure it anyway).

**Candidate B**: Extract `Math.ceil` call into a named helper like `roundUpToGold`. Rejected — `Math.ceil` is universally readable as "round up"; wrapping adds an element with no clarity gain.

**Candidate C**: Move `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` near `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` since both encode pricing data. Currently they're separated by `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`. Marginal clarity gain — reorder constants by domain grouping (pricing data together, then surcharges/fees).

**Candidate D**: After cycle 11 added the moonstone test which confirmed the existing code works without modification, the structure is unchanged. The 11th test serves the same role as test 9 (4 runes) — confirming the narrow `every("rune")` guard correctly excludes mixed-type cases. **This is the THIRD confirming test for the narrow guard.** No structural refactoring forced; cycle 12 is still the genuine forcing test.

Let me consider Candidate C more carefully — grouping by domain.

Current order:
```
PROCESSING_FEE
FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE  
THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE
BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE
```

A more domain-coherent grouping would put the pricing data together (base prices and block price) since both are item-pricing data, separate from premium-formula constants (rate, fee):

```
BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE       // per-item base prices
THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE  // alternative pricing for a bundle
FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE  // premium-formula multiplier
PROCESSING_FEE           // premium-formula constant
```

This reflects the flow of the function: base prices → block override → surcharge → fee. Minor Rule 2 improvement.

However, this is purely cosmetic and changes no semantics, no mass. Let me consider whether there's a more substantive improvement.

**Candidate E**: Eliminate the duplicated `25` in `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` by deriving moonstone from rune. Rejected — would falsely couple two distinct types via implementation detail.

**Candidate F**: The `items.every((item) => item.type === "rune")` predicate now sits next to `moonstone: 25` in the type table. The visual juxtaposition of "moonstone exists as a type but is excluded from the rune block" is somewhat awkward. Renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` to `isThreeOfTheSameRuneType` doesn't add clarity. Renaming `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` is not warranted yet.

Given all these candidates, the most defensible micro-improvement is **Candidate C** (constant grouping by domain role) — purely cosmetic, no mass change, minor Rule 2 win in code-reading flow. But this is genuinely marginal.

Let me reconsider. The architecture notes show three consecutive "no refactoring" cycles (5, 10) are acceptable when justified. After cycle 11, the situation is structurally identical to cycle 10: a confirming test for the narrow guard, no new abstraction unlocked, and the genuine forcing test is the very next one (cycle 12). I'll apply Candidate C as the minor cosmetic regroup since it's a legitimate Rule 2 improvement and the mandate is to attempt at least one refactoring.

## Step 5: Implement Refactoring (Candidate C)

content.text:
All 11 tests still green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

Mass change: **0** — pure reordering, identical token count, identical control flow.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Reordered module-level constants by domain role: pricing data (`BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE`) grouped first, then premium-formula constants (`FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `PROCESSING_FEE`).
- Reflects the function's computational flow: lookup base prices → maybe apply block override → apply surcharge → add fee.
- Tests: 11/11 passing.
- Mass change: 0 (no semantic change, pure reordering).
- Rule 2 (reveals intent): minor improvement — domain-coherent grouping makes the data shape easier to scan when adding cycle 12's per-type block logic.

## Step 7: Append to Architecture Notes

content.content:
44	## After cycle 10
45	- No refactoring (justified): the 7-runes test (expected base 175 = 7 × 25, premium 198) passed without any implementation change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `items.length === 3` excludes the 7-rune case from the block-discount path, so `rawBasePrice` (175) flows straight through `basePrice` and the existing formula yields ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = ceil(197.5) = 198. Important observation: the test's prose ("1 block of 3 + 4 individuals → 175 G base") describes the *narrative* but the *numeric expectation* (175 = 7 × 25, no discount) confirms that the block discount only applies when exactly 3 runes are insured — making this the SECOND confirming test for the narrow guard, not a forcing test. The genuine forcing test for `floor(runeCount/3)` generalisation is cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base"), where two distinct per-type blocks must each be priced at 60. Generalising now (computing `floor(count/3)` blocks across all runes only) would still need restructuring once moonstones arrive, so it's premature. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock` (consequence-oriented over structure-oriented, marginal Rule 2 gain) — rejected because cycle 12 will force the predicate to change shape entirely (per-type rune+moonstone block counting), so any rename now is churn. Considered inlining `rawBasePrice` into the ternary or collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * 1.1` — both rejected on cycle-4/cycle-5 precedent that test-name vocabulary ("base + first-insurance + fee") earns each value its own named binding (Rule 2 > APP). Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function — rejected on cycle-8 precedent (used once, inlining preserves locality). All existing names still fit.
46

## After cycle 11
- Change: reordered module-level constants by domain role. Now grouped as `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (with the new `moonstone: 25` entry added in Green) + `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` first (item-pricing data), followed by `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` + `PROCESSING_FEE` (premium-formula constants). Body of `quote` unchanged.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the new ordering mirrors the function's computational flow (lookup base prices → maybe apply block override → apply surcharge → add fee), making the data shape easier to scan as cycle 12 will introduce per-type block-pricing logic. Mass change: 0 (pure reorder). The "2 runes + 1 moonstone → no block" test is the THIRD confirming test for the narrow `isThreeAlikeRunes` guard (after cycles 9 and 10): the `every((item) => item.type === "rune")` predicate correctly excludes the mixed-type case, so no structural change was needed. Cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base") remains the genuine forcing test that will restructure the predicate into per-type block counting. Considered (and rejected) generalising the predicate now — would be triangulation on three confirming negatives. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a function or renaming it — rejected on cycle-8/10 precedent (used once, cycle-12 will reshape it). Considered deduplicating `rune: 25, moonstone: 25` via a shared constant — rejected as false coupling (the two types share a price by spec coincidence, not by definition). All existing names still fit.
- Naming established: none new; constant order is the only change.
